reference: General American-football concepts. High-level tendencies for understanding a broadcast — not certainties, not team-specific.

personnel.name: 10 personnel
personnel.detail: 1 RB, 0 TE, 4 WR. Spread-out pass look; often empty or four-wide. Signals pass-lean and tempo; defense counters with a nickel/dime and lighter box.

personnel.name: 11 personnel
personnel.detail: 1 RB, 1 TE, 3 WR. The modern base — balanced, keeps run and pass both live. Defense usually answers with nickel (5 DBs).

personnel.name: 12 personnel
personnel.detail: 1 RB, 2 TE, 2 WR. Bigger, run-friendly but a strong play-action base; two TEs stress the edges. Defense may stay in base or bring an extra box defender.

personnel.name: 21 personnel
personnel.detail: 2 RB (one often a fullback), 1 TE, 2 WR. Downhill run and lead-blocking; classic short-yardage and play-action set.

personnel.name: 22 personnel
personnel.detail: 2 RB, 2 TE, 1 WR. Heaviest common grouping — short-yardage, goal line, or milking the clock. Expect a loaded box.

personnel.name: 13 personnel
personnel.detail: 1 RB, 3 TE, 1 WR. Heavy, gap-scheme run and boot-action pass; unbalanced looks are common.

formations.name: I-formation
formations.detail: QB under center, fullback and running back stacked behind. Downhill run and lead blocking; sets up play-action off the run threat.

formations.name: Singleback
formations.detail: QB under center or in gun with one back. Balanced; the back can run either way and the offense can flood the field with receivers.

formations.name: Shotgun
formations.detail: QB ~5 yards deep to catch the snap. Pass-friendly, better sightlines, quicker three-step timing; still supports zone-read and draw runs.

formations.name: Pistol
formations.detail: QB ~4 yards back with the RB directly behind. A hybrid — keeps downhill run identity of under-center while giving the QB a gun-style read.

formations.name: Empty
formations.detail: No back beside the QB — five eligible receivers spread out. Almost always pass; forces the defense to declare coverage and leaves the QB to handle protection reads.

formations.name: Trips
formations.detail: Three receivers to one side. Stresses the coverage numbers to that side and isolates the backside receiver one-on-one.

formations.name: Bunch
formations.detail: Three receivers clustered tight together. Creates natural rubs/picks and is hard to press; usually beats man coverage.

formations.name: Twins
formations.detail: Two receivers to one side. A balanced two-by-two look that keeps route combinations simple and reads clean.

coverages.name: Cover 0
coverages.spot: No deep safety — everybody is manned up and there is often an extra rusher.
coverages.weakness: One broken tackle or beaten man and there is no help behind it; beaten by quick, decisive throws and mobile QBs.
coverages.detail: All-out man pressure. High risk, high reward — the offense wants a quick answer or a big play.

coverages.name: Cover 1
coverages.spot: One deep safety in the middle, man coverage underneath, often a free rusher or a robber lurking.
coverages.weakness: Man-beating routes — crossers, mesh, pick concepts — and the QB's legs if the rush is contained.
coverages.detail: Man-free. The single high safety is the only deep help, so receivers must be beaten one-on-one.

coverages.name: Cover 2
coverages.spot: Two deep safeties splitting the field in half, five underneath zone defenders.
coverages.weakness: The deep middle between the safeties and the sideline behind the corner (the honey hole); also runs, with only seven in the box.
coverages.detail: Two-deep zone. Corners jam and sink; strong against the short and intermediate outside.

coverages.name: Cover 3
coverages.spot: Three deep defenders each take a third of the field; four underneath.
coverages.weakness: Flood the underneath — four verticals stresses the deep thirds, and out-breaking and flat routes soften the short zones.
coverages.detail: The most common zone shell. One high safety, corners bail to deep thirds; sound against deep balls, softer underneath.

coverages.name: Cover 4 (quarters)
coverages.spot: Four deep defenders each take a quarter; safeties read and can jump routes or fit the run.
coverages.weakness: Underneath and the flats early; a good run look because safeties can trigger downhill.
coverages.detail: Quarters. Built to take away deep shots and rally to the run; underneath windows are open before the safeties fall off.

coverages.name: Cover 6
coverages.spot: Quarters to one side, Cover 2 to the other — the field is split (roughly a quarter-quarter-half look).
coverages.weakness: Attacking the seam between the two coverage rules, and knowing which side is soft.
coverages.detail: A split-field answer that lets the defense play different rules to the field and the boundary.

coverages.name: Tampa 2
coverages.spot: Cover 2 look but the middle linebacker sprints to the deep middle, closing the Cover 2 hole.
coverages.weakness: The flats and the sidelines, and quick underneath throws before the middle closes.
coverages.detail: A Cover 2 variant that fixes the deep-middle vulnerability by dropping the Mike deep.

pressures.name: Zone blitz
pressures.detail: Bring extra rushers but drop a lineman into coverage, keeping a zone shell behind it. Confuses protections while staying sound; often a five-man rush with three-deep, three-under behind it.

pressures.name: Fire zone
pressures.detail: The classic five-man zone pressure — overload one side, drop the backside end, play three-deep three-under. Pressure without gambling the whole coverage.

pressures.name: Corner/nickel blitz
pressures.detail: A defensive back rushes off the edge, usually from the slot. Fast pressure at the QB's blind or throwing side; leaves that receiver's area for someone to rotate to.

pressures.name: Double-A-gap
pressures.detail: Two linebackers walk up over the center's inside gaps pre-snap. Threatens interior pressure and forces the protection to declare; often a bluff that bails at the snap.

pressures.name: Simulated pressure
pressures.detail: Show blitz, rush only four. Muddies the QB's protection math and post-snap read without giving up an extra coverage defender.

tendencies.name: 1st and 10
tendencies.detail: The most balanced down; run and play-action both live. Defenses stay base/nickel and honest. A run here sets up the play-action shot later.

tendencies.name: 2nd and short (1-3)
tendencies.detail: Offense-friendly — the whole playbook is open, so it is a prime play-action and shot-play down. Defenses must respect the run, which springs the pass.

tendencies.name: 2nd and long (8+)
tendencies.detail: Pass-leaning but not desperate; draws, screens, and check-downs are common to set up a manageable third down. Defense plays coverage and rushes four.

tendencies.name: 3rd and short (1-2)
tendencies.detail: Run or quick-game lean; heavier personnel and a loaded box are common. Defense sells out to stop the sticks; play-action off it can hit big.

tendencies.name: 3rd and medium (3-6)
tendencies.detail: The true chess match — sticks routes, option routes, and pick concepts vs. man; defense mixes coverage and may bring pressure.

tendencies.name: 3rd and long (7+)
tendencies.detail: Clear pass down. Expect the QB in gun/empty, a four- or five-man rush, and zone behind it; the offense targets the sticks and hopes for a scramble drill.

tendencies.name: Red zone
tendencies.detail: Field shrinks, deep routes die, windows tighten. Fades, slants, picks, and quick outs rule; defenses play more man and tighter coverage. Turnovers and pressure spike in value.

tendencies.name: Goal line
tendencies.detail: Heavy personnel, power runs, play-action off it, and back-shoulder fades. Defense loads the box with base or goal-line personnel.

tendencies.name: Backed up (own 1-10)
tendencies.detail: Conservative — safe runs and short passes to earn breathing room; defense may pressure hoping for a safety or a short field.

tendencies.name: Two-minute drill
tendencies.detail: Tempo, sideline routes to stop the clock, spike threat, and clock/timeout management. Defense plays soft zone (prevent) to keep everything in front and force underneath completions.

tendencies.name: Four-minute (offense leading late)
tendencies.detail: Run the ball, stay in bounds, burn clock. Defense must stop the run and use timeouts; expect heavier personnel from the offense.

glossary.def: The count of running backs and tight ends on the field, given as two digits (e.g. 11 = 1 RB, 1 TE, so 3 WR).
The area near the line of scrimmage between the tackles; 'loaded box' means extra defenders there to stop the run.
How many safeties are deep. Single-high (one deep) hints man or Cover 1/3; two-high hints Cover 2/4 or a disguise.
In man each defender covers a player; in zone each covers an area and passes receivers off. Rubs beat man; flooding beats zone.
A fake handoff to freeze linebackers and safeties, opening throwing windows behind them. Most dangerous when the run is a real threat.
Run-pass option — the QB reads one defender after the snap and either hands off or throws based on how that defender reacts.
A player shifting before the snap to reveal coverage (a defender following = man), create leverage, or change the numbers to a side.
A receiver's built-in quick answer versus a blitz — a short throw that beats the extra rusher.
The first-down line; 'at the sticks' means a route breaking right around the yardage needed.
Which side of a receiver a defender lines up on, telling you where help is and which way he wants to funnel the route.
